claims.claim: At 100.8 kPa, the average ignition time for single batteries decreased with increasing SOC: 364 s at 0% SOC, 342 s at 50% SOC, and 315 s at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the average time to ignition decreases with the increasing SOC under the same radiation exposure... the average ignition time in Hefei is longer than in Lhasa

claims.claim: At lower ambient pressure (64.3 kPa, Lhasa), batteries ignited faster than at higher ambient pressure (100.8 kPa, Hefei) under the same heating conditions.
claims.quote: The ejection in Lhasa will appear earlier than that in Hefei due to its lower pressure required for the rupture... the increasing of the altitude or reduction in ambient pressure causes the battery to be ignited faster

claims.claim: Peak HRR values for single batteries at 100.8 kPa were 2.86, 5.1, and 2.84 kW for 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC respectively, showing a non-monotonic relationship with SOC.
claims.quote: The peak HRRs values corresponding to 0%, 50% and 100% SOC were 2.86, 5.1 and 2.84 kW respectively at 100.8 kPa

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the CO/CO2 ratio increased sharply compared to lower SOC values, indicating a significant decrease in combustion efficiency at full charge.
claims.quote: The sharp increase in CO/CO2 ratio of 100% SOC battery indicates an obvious decrease in combustion efﬁciency.

claims.claim: The total heat release of bundled batteries increases with cell number in a power function Q(n) = an^b, where the coefficient a is pressure dependent: a = 16.99 at 64.3 kPa and a = 22.57 at 100.8 kPa.
claims.quote: The total heat release in the bundle increases with the battery numbers in a power function. The coefﬁcient of the proportionality is pressure dependent... Value 16.99 [a at 64.3 kPa]... 22.57 [a at 100.8 kPa]

claims.claim: At 0% SOC with 400-W heating power and at 100% SOC with 100-W heating power, only the SVO minor pressure occurred with no thermal runaway or intense major impact pressure.
claims.quote: Tests of a 0% SOC battery with 400-W heating power and a 100% SOC battery with 100-W heating power were also conducted, but both had only the SVO minor pressure and no thermal runaway or intense major impact pressure.

claims.claim: The maximum impact pressure during the intense ejection stage increased with increasing battery SOC, reaching 10.8, 11.9, 19.2, 86.5, and 107.2 kPa at SOCs of 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100%, respectively, under 400-W heating power.
claims.quote: The ejection of the thermal runaway generated maximum overpressures of 10.8, 11.9, and 19.2 kPa when the battery SOC was of 20%, 40%, and 60%, respectively. However, thermal runaway occurred dramatically at a high SOC with the maximum impact pressure soaring to 86.5 and 107.2 kPa when the battery SOCs were 80% and 100%, respectively.

claims.claim: The maximum pressure-rise rate during the intense ejection stage increased dramatically at high SOC: rates were 42.6, 64.6, and 49.2 kPa/s at 20%, 40%, and 60% SOC, but soared to 673.6 and 694.4 kPa/s at 80% and 100% SOC, respectively, under 400-W heating power.
claims.quote: the maximum pressure-rise rates were 42.6, 64.6, and 49.2 kPa∙s−1 ... when the thermal runaway occurred at a 20%, 40%, and 60% SOC, respectively. However, the maximum rates soared to 673.6 and 694.4 kPa∙s−1 ... with an 80% and 100% SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: The initial pressure-rise temperature and maximum pressure temperature during the intense ejection stage declined with increasing battery SOC, reaching as low as 241.6 °C and 241.8 °C respectively at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the initial pressure-rise temperatures (TIPR) reached 318.3, 285.2, 273.4, 262.3, and 241.6 °C, and the maximum pressure temperatures (TMP) were 328.1, 286.6, 276.0, 263.5, and 241.8 °C with battery SOCs of 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100%, respectively. The initial pressure-rise temperature and the maximum pressure temperature declined with an increase in the battery SOC.

claims.claim: At SOCs of 20%, 40%, and 60% with 400-W heating power, the injury risk from impact pressure was below the LD1 curve, while at all other tested conditions (higher SOC or different heating powers) the injury risk was above LD1 and below LD10, with the result close to LD10 at 100% SOC heated at 350 W or 400 W.
claims.quote: the injury risk of the impact pressures from thermal runaway with LIB SOCs of 20%, 40%, and 60% and heating power of 400 W were below the LD1 curve. However, the injury risk of all other conditions was above the LD1 curve and below the LD10 curve. The test result was close to the LD10 curve when a 100% SOC battery was externally heated at 350 W or 400 W.

claims.claim: The average temperature at which cells start safety venting was 193°C under a heating ramp of 11°C/min.
claims.quote: The average temperature where the cells start the safety venting was found at 193 ◦C.

claims.claim: The average exothermic onset temperature across all nine experiments was 238°C.
claims.quote: The exothermic onset was, on average, 238 ◦C.

claims.claim: The average maximum temperature achieved on the cell surface across the nine experiments was 567°C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature achieved in the cell surface was average for the nine experiments, 567 ◦C.

claims.claim: LCO initiated flame by a hot spot in the vent cap, while NMC ejected hot solid particles that ignited the flammable gas; LFP did not show any flame propagation for any of the three SOC levels tested.
claims.quote: The high-speed camera recording shows that the LCO initiated the flame by a hot spot in the vent cap while the NMC ejected hot solid particles that burned the flammable gas. The LFP did not show any flame propagation for the three SOC tested.

claims.claim: Increasing SOC decreases the venting temperature and increases the maximum self-heat rate for LCO, NMC, and LFP cells.
claims.quote: The temperature at the start of the thermal runaway decreased, and the rate of temperature rise in the cell increased with SOC.
